title: Whittled apple bottle stopper
subtitle: Small carved stopper for a wine or oil bottle
excerpt: A cork-replacement bottle stopper carved from apple wood. The plug is the size of a standard cork and the top is a hand-carved knob. A short whittling project that introduces the round-over and the shoulder cut without committing to a full spoon.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for small treen turning and carving. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry on hand-shaped cylindrical components.

glossaryTerms.definition: The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, cutting off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short vertical cut into the wood that stops the next push or pull cut, preventing the grain from tearing past the intended end-point.

content.text: A whittled bottle stopper replaces a worn cork: a 25 mm cylindrical plug at the base, a square shoulder, and a carved knob on top. Apple is the recommended wood. The grain is tight, the colour is pale cream ageing to honey, and the dense fibres compress slightly to seal against the inside of the bottle neck. The piece can be carved from a small offcut, green or seasoned. Both work; seasoned wood is easier to size accurately because it will not shrink further on the shelf.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one; the dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

A short apple billet: 8 cm long, 4 cm square, or any small offcut that contains a cylinder of 25 mm diameter and 30 mm height plus enough wood above for a 35 mm knob. Apple windfall is the typical source. Community orchards in autumn yield offcuts and prunings; the local tree-surgeon's pile is the reliable winter source. Cherry, beech, and sycamore are good alternatives with the same fine grain.
Tools
A
sloyd knife

for the bulk of the work, an optional detail knife for the knob edges, a leather strop, and pure tung oil for the finish. Pure tung is chosen here because the stopper repeatedly touches liquid: it sets harder than raw linseed and is more water-resistant once fully set.
Method

Measure the bottle. A standard wine bottle takes a plug 19 to 21 mm in diameter; a typical olive-oil bottle takes 22 to 25 mm. Measure with a ruler held across the neck opening. The numbers in this method assume a 21 mm wine-bottle neck; scale up or down by a millimetre or two as needed. The
push cut
is the default throughout. Strop every 15 minutes.
Mark the four zones on the
blank

with pencil: the plug (25 mm long, 25 mm square at this stage), the shoulder (5 mm tall, 30 mm square), the knob neck (10 mm tall, 18 mm square), and the knob top (25 mm tall, 35 mm square). Pencil the boundaries on all four faces.
Cut a
stop cut

at the shoulder boundary on each face: a 2 mm deep vertical cut at the line between the plug and the shoulder. The stop cut prevents the next push cut from tearing past it into the shoulder. Repeat at the boundary between shoulder and knob neck.

Reduce the plug zone to a cylinder. Work in 16 facets: cut a thin facet down each face to the stop cut, rotate, repeat. After four facets the plug is an octagon; another eight cuts make it a smooth 16-sided cylinder. Refine to a circle with thumb-pivot cuts.

Check the plug fits the bottle. Try it; it should slide in 5 mm and then bind. Pare back the diameter with light push cuts and try again. The aim is a firm push fit: the plug seats by hand pressure alone but does not slip out when the bottle is inverted. Apple compresses slightly so a snug fit is correct. A loose fit is a recarved plug.

Reduce the knob neck the same way: 16 facets to an octagon then to a smooth cylinder, 18 mm diameter. The shoulder between plug and knob neck remains square, slightly proud at the corners, which seats the stopper against the bottle neck and prevents it sliding down.

Shape the knob. A turned-egg shape is the traditional form: round-over the four top corners first, then refine to an oval that sits comfortably between thumb and forefinger. Plan for a 32 to 35 mm diameter at the widest part.
Soften the shoulder corners with thumb-pivot cuts so they do not bite into the bottle rim. Keep the underside of the shoulder flat; it must sit flush on the bottle.
Finishing

Sand through 120, 180, then 240 grit on the plug only; the plug needs a smooth surface to seat. The knob can be left tool-marked or sanded to taste. Apply pure tung oil in three thin coats: wipe on, wait an hour, wipe off the surplus, leave to cure for 2 days between coats. Full cure for water-contact use is 3 weeks; the stopper can be used dry sooner, but lasts longer once fully cured.
Variations
Tall narrow knob: replace the egg with a 50 mm long taper for a tall-bottle aesthetic.
Cherry or beech instead of apple: same method and dimensions. Cherry warms to a deeper pink; beech stays pale.
Decanter stopper: a longer plug (35 mm) and a flatter knob for the shape of a decanter neck.
Care

Rinse the plug under warm water if it touches food-contact liquid (oil, vinegar); a small amount of washing-up liquid is fine if needed. Towel-dry immediately and leave to air on a shelf overnight before returning to the bottle. Re-oil the plug surface every six months.

items.symptom: Plug is too loose; falls out of the bottle
items.cause: Too many facets cut on the cylinder; over-pared diameter
items.fix: Start over with a fresh plug section. Stop short of a perfect cylinder; the plug should feel just-too-large before the final smoothing.

items.symptom: Plug splits along the grain when pushed into the bottle
items.cause: Grain runs across the plug rather than along its length
items.fix: Cut the next plug so the grain runs along the length of the plug. End-grain plugs split; long-grain plugs do not.

items.symptom: Shoulder does not sit flush; stopper wobbles
items.cause: Underside of the shoulder cut at an angle
items.fix: Pare the underside flat with the knife held parallel to the workbench surface. Test on a flat block before fitting to the bottle.

items.symptom: Plug sticks and is hard to remove
items.cause: Fresh-cut wood swelling slightly with bottle moisture
items.fix: Withdraw, dry, and reduce the diameter by a millimetre with light push cuts. Allow the cure to complete before retrying.
